Electrical Signature Analysis

Every AC motor tells you what's wrong. Through its electrical signature.

Current and voltage waveforms captured at the motor control cabinet reveal the condition of the entire drivetrain. SAM4 reads those signatures 24/7. No sensors on the machine.

Most condition monitoring requires sensors on the machine. ESA doesn't. That's why it works where vibration, thermal, and ultrasound can't.

Request a Demo See Detection Capabilities ↓
  • The Physics
  • Data Acquisition
  • Signal Processing
  • AI & Diagnostics
  • Detection Capabilities
  • Boundaries
The Physics

Mechanical faults change the electrical signature of a motor.

Every AC induction motor draws current from the supply. That current creates a rotating magnetic field in the stator, which induces current in the rotor. When something goes wrong mechanically, it shows up in the electrical signal.

1

Motor draws current

The motor draws AC current from the supply. The interaction between stator and rotor magnetic fields produces torque that drives the load.

2

Fault changes torque

A worn bearing increases friction. Misalignment creates cyclic load variation. Cavitation alters the hydraulic profile. Each fault changes the torque demand.

3

Torque changes waveform

The air gap between stator and rotor acts as a transducer. Mechanical changes produce measurable modulations in current and voltage waveforms.

4

ESA reads the pattern

Each fault type creates a distinct spectral fingerprint. SAM4 reads those patterns continuously from the motor control cabinet.

The core principle: The electrical signature of a motor contains the mechanical condition of the entire drivetrain. You don't need a sensor on the machine. You need a sensor on the electrical supply.

The physics has been validated for over 40 years. What's new is the ability to read these signatures continuously, at scale, with AI models trained on thousands of assets.

Data Acquisition

From current clamp to cloud. No sensors on the machine.

SAM4 captures current and voltage waveforms at the motor control cabinet using non-intrusive current clamps and voltage taps. Sampling rates up to 64 kHz. Installation takes less than 60 minutes per asset. Motor returns to service the same shift.

1

Current Clamps

Split-core CTs clamp around the motor supply cables in the MCC. Requires a de-energized cabinet. Back online the next shift.

2

DAQ Unit

The Samotics data acquisition unit samples three-phase current and voltage at high frequency. Mounts inside or adjacent to the MCC panel.

3

Cellular Gateway

Data transmits via secure cellular connection. No dependency on plant IT/OT networks. No firewall changes.

4

Cloud Processing

Signal processing runs on edge hardware and in the Samotics cloud. AI classification and diagnostic review follow. No data science team required on your side.

Signal Processing

Turning raw waveforms into fault signatures.

Unlike vibration or thermal data, electrical signals capture mechanical, electrical, and operational behaviour in a single measurement. One sensor point. Three diagnostic dimensions.

FFT and Spectral Analysis

Raw three-phase current and voltage waveforms are captured at high frequency. Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) converts time-domain waveforms into frequency-domain spectra. This reveals the harmonic content that characterizes fault signatures.

Each fault type — bearing wear, misalignment, rotor bar damage, cavitation — creates distinct patterns in specific frequency bands. Bearing faults appear as modulations around running speed. Misalignment shows up in even harmonics. Rotor bar faults appear as sidebands around slip frequency.

How Baselines Work

SAM4 establishes a spectral baseline during the 2–4 week calibration period. This baseline captures the "healthy" spectrum for the specific motor, load, and operating conditions.

Ongoing monitoring compares live spectra against this baseline. Deviations become the anomaly signal — increases in specific frequency bands flag developing faults before mechanical failure occurs.

What the Analysis Extracts

  • Rotational speed and load profile
  • Bearing fault frequencies (BSF, BPFO, BPFI)
  • Misalignment indicators (even harmonics)
  • Rotor bar condition (rotor slot harmonics)
  • Stator winding integrity (inter-turn faults)
AI & Diagnostics

AI identifies patterns. Engineers validate root cause.

Anomaly detection alone generates too many false positives. SAM4 uses a three-stage pipeline: statistical anomaly flagging, AI classification by fault type, and final validation by a Samotics engineer.

1

Anomaly Detection

Statistical models flag deviations from spectral baseline. Changes beyond normal operating variance trigger the next stage.

2

Fault Classification

AI models trained on 10,000+ assets classify the anomaly by fault type: bearing, alignment, balance, electrical, or process.

3

Engineer Validation

A Samotics reliability engineer reviews every classified fault before it reaches your team. Human validation prevents false alarms.

Detection Capabilities

What ESA detects

SAM4 identifies developing faults across the full drivetrain. Detection lead times range from weeks to months before failure.

Fault Type Component Detection Method Typical Lead Time
Mechanical Faults
Bearing Wear* Motor/pump bearings Ball pass frequency outer race (BPFO), ball pass frequency inner race (BPFI) 8–12 weeks
Misalignment Coupling Even harmonics, asynchronous sidebands 6–10 weeks
Imbalance Rotor Running speed harmonic (1X) elevation 4–8 weeks
Coupling Wear Flexible coupling Running speed harmonics + sidebands 6–12 weeks
Impeller Damage Pump/fan impeller Blade pass frequency + sidebands 4–10 weeks
Rotor Eccentricity Motor rotor Slot harmonics modulation 8–16 weeks
Electrical Faults
Winding Insulation Degradation Motor stator High-frequency transient detection, ground leakage 6–20 weeks
Broken Rotor Bars Motor rotor Slip-frequency sidebands around stator harmonics 4–12 weeks
Power Quality Issues Electrical supply Harmonic distortion, voltage imbalance Real-time
Process Faults
Cavitation Pump Broadband noise elevation, high-frequency spectrum 2–8 weeks
Blockage/Restriction Filter, screen, line Load profile increase, current elevation 1–4 weeks
Thermal Runaway Motor windings Sustained current elevation, waveform distortion Hours to days
Lubrication Degradation Bearings, gearbox Bearing fault frequencies + elevated baseline 4–16 weeks

*Bearing detection accuracy varies by position: 45.9% overall across all bearing locations, 30.8% for pump/motor bearings specifically. ESA detects bearing faults earlier in the drivetrain (gearbox, coupling-side) more reliably than at the driven end. Core three fault categories (blockage, mechanical unbalance, cavitation) detect at 90.8%.

Honest Boundaries

Where ESA works best — and where it doesn't

Best Suited For

  • Submerged, enclosed, or hazardous assets
  • Fleet-wide monitoring from a single MCC
  • Continuous 24/7 surveillance without downtime
  • Electrical fault detection (insulation, phase imbalance)
  • Assets where vibration sensors are impractical
  • Gearboxes, pumps, compressors, centrifuges

Constraints to Know

  • VFD-driven motors: inverter switching noise reduces resolution for bearing fault frequencies and rotor bar sidebands. Mechanical unbalance and misalignment detection unaffected.
  • Minimum RPM requirements (typically 300+ RPM for reliable detection)
  • MCC physical access is mandatory for clamp installation
  • 2–4 week calibration period before full detection capability
  • High-slip motors (cranes, hoists) may need longer observation windows
  • Three-phase power required (single-phase motors not supported)

See what ESA reveals about your assets

A 30-minute demo walks through the full pipeline — from signal capture to validated diagnostic — running on real industrial assets.

At first I was a bit sceptical about SAM4's ability to detect failures by analysing electrical waveforms, but the results speak for themselves: it just works. DuPont
